abstract:
Title: Comparative Analysis of Laparoscopic vs Open Appendectomy for Reducing Wound Infection in Adults with Acute Appendicitis

Background:
Acute appendicitis is a common gastrointestinal emergency requiring prompt surgical intervention. Traditional open appendectomy is the gold standard, but increasing evidence suggests that laparoscopic appendectomy may offer comparable outcomes with reduced morbidity.

Methods:
We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing laparoscopic appendectomy to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. Twelve RCTs were included, totaling 1,432 participants. Primary outcome measures were wound infection rates and postoperative complications.

Results:
Laparoscopic appendectomy was associated with reduced wound infection rates compared to open appendectomy (RR: 0.35, 95% CI: 0.24-0.51). The odds ratio for postoperative complication was also lower in the laparoscopic group (OR: 0.57, 95% CI: 0.39-0.81), although this difference did not reach statistical significance. Standardized mean difference (SMD) for wound infection rates at 30 days post-surgery showed a significant reduction in laparoscopic appendectomy (SMD: -0.83, 95% CI: -1.26 to -0.40).

Conclusion:
Our meta-analysis suggests that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with reduced wound infection rates and lower postoperative complications compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. These findings support the use of minimally invasive surgical techniques as a viable alternative to traditional open surgery, potentially reducing morbidity and improving patient outcomes.

abstract:
Background: Open appendectomy is a traditional surgical approach for treating acute appendicitis, but it has been associated with increased rates of wound infection compared to laparoscopic appendectomy.

Methods: A randomized controlled trial (RCT) was conducted at a single center between 2015 and 2020. Adults with confirmed acute appendicitis were randomly assigned to undergo either open or laparoscopic appendectomy. Wound complications, including infections, were recorded and analyzed using standardized definitions.

Results: Of the 100 participants enrolled in the trial, 52 underwent open appendectomy and 48 underwent laparoscopic appendectomy. The incidence of wound infection was significantly lower among those who received laparoscopic appendectomy (4.2%) compared to open appendectomy (15.4%). The relative risk of wound infection after laparoscopic appendectomy was 0.33 (95% CI: 0.14-0.76, p = 0.005).

Conclusion: Our results suggest that laparoscopic appendectomy reduces the risk of wound infection compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. These findings are consistent with existing evidence and provide further support for the use of minimally invasive surgical techniques in this context. The significant reduction in wound complications may have implications for patient recovery, healthcare resource utilization, and quality of life. However, further studies are needed to confirm these results and explore potential benefits and limitations of laparoscopic appendectomy in different populations.

Background:
Acute appendicitis is a common surgical emergency requiring prompt intervention. Open appendectomy has been the traditional standard of care, but laparoscopic appendectomy (LA) has gained popularity due to its minimally invasive nature.

Methods:
We conducted a randomized controlled trial comparing LA with open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. A total of 1,200 patients were randomly assigned to either group. All participants received perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis and standard care post-operatively. Wound infection rates were assessed at 30 days post-surgery.

Results:
The wound infection rate was significantly lower in the LA group (3.4%) compared to the open appendectomy group (7.8%) (RR: 0.43, 95% CI: 0.27-0.67). Odds ratio for wound infection was also significantly reduced in the LA group (OR: 0.36, 95% CI: 0.19-0.68). Standardized mean difference between wound infection rates between the two groups was -1.43 (95% CI: -2.21 to -0.65).

Conclusion:
Our findings suggest that laparoscopic appendectomy reduces wound infection compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. The significantly lower wound infection rate in the LA group supports the use of minimally invasive surgical techniques for this condition, which may result in reduced morbidity and faster recovery times.

Title: Laparoscopic Appendectomy vs Open Appendectomy for Acute Appendicitis: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis

Background: Appendicitis is a common surgical condition in adults, often managed with either laparoscopic or open appendectomy. The choice between these approaches can significantly impact postoperative outcomes.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing laparoscopic appendectomy to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. We included RCTs that reported wound infection rates as primary or secondary outcomes. A random-effects model was used to estimate the relative risk of wound infection between groups.

Results: Eighteen RCTs were identified, involving a total of 2,531 participants. The pooled relative risk of wound infection in laparoscopic appendectomy vs open appendectomy was 0.45 (95% CI: 0.35-0.57), indicating a significant reduction in wound infection with laparoscopic approach.

Standardized mean difference analysis revealed a similar result, with a standardized mean difference of -1.23 (95% CI: -1.63 to -0.83) in favor of laparoscopic appendectomy. The odds ratio for wound infection was also significantly lower in the laparoscopic group (OR: 0.42; 95% CI: 0.31-0.59).

Conclusion: Our meta-analysis suggests that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with a significant reduction in wound infection compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. These findings support the use of minimally invasive surgical techniques for managing acute appendicitis, potentially improving patient outcomes and reducing postoperative complications.

Title: Laparoscopic Appendectomy vs Open Appendectomy in Adults with Acute Appendicitis: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis

Background: Acute appendicitis is a common cause of abdominal pain, often requiring surgical intervention. The choice between laparoscopic (minimally invasive) and open appendectomy has been debated due to concerns about postoperative complications.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials and observational studies comparing the outcomes of laparoscopic and open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. We searched electronic databases up to 2022 and included studies that reported wound infection rates as primary or secondary outcomes.

Results: Our analysis consisted of 12 studies involving 1,455 patients. The pooled relative risk of wound infection in the laparoscopic group was 0.64 (95% CI: 0.44, 0.93), indicating a significant reduction in wound infections compared to open appendectomy (RR = 0.64, OR = 0.67). Additionally, we found that the standardized mean difference of wound infection rates between laparoscopic and open appendectomy was -0.33 (95% CI: -0.57, -0.09).

Conclusion: Our meta-analysis suggests that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with a lower rate of wound infections compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. These findings support the use of minimally invasive surgical techniques for this condition, potentially reducing postoperative complications and improving patient outcomes.

Title: Laparoscopic Appendectomy versus Open Appendectomy for Acute Appendicitis: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Wound Infection Rates

Background: Acute appendicitis is a common cause of abdominal surgery, with laparoscopic (minimally invasive) and open appendectomies being two widely used surgical approaches. The safety of these procedures in preventing wound infections has not been conclusively established.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs), observational studies, and prospective cohort studies evaluating the comparison between laparoscopic and open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. Outcomes were wound infection rates, defined as surgical site infections (SSIs) or superficial incisional infections (SIIs).

Results: Our meta-analysis included 15 RCTs, 21 observational studies, and 14 prospective cohort studies. The pooled estimate of relative risk for laparoscopic appendectomy versus open appendectomy was 0.63 (95% CI: 0.46-0.87). Odds ratio estimates were similar (OR 0.64, 95% CI: 0.46-0.89), while the standardized mean difference for wound infection rates was -0.34 (95% CI: -0.61 to -0.07).

Conclusion: Our meta-analysis suggests that laparoscopic appendectomy may be associated with reduced wound infection rates compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. While the current evidence does not rule out potential risks and complications of laparoscopic surgery, these findings support the use of minimally invasive techniques when feasible, potentially reducing morbidity and healthcare costs associated with surgical site infections.

Title: Laparoscopic Appendectomy vs Open Appendectomy for Acute Appendicitis: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis

Background: Surgical management of acute appendicitis is a common medical condition that requires careful consideration of surgical technique to minimize post-operative complications.

Methods: This systematic review and meta-analysis pooled data from 15 randomized controlled trials comparing laparoscopic appendectomy (LA) to open appendectomy (OA). A total of 3,444 patients were included in the analysis. The primary outcome was wound infection after surgery.

Results: Results showed that LA significantly reduced wound infection compared to OA (RR = 0.35, 95% CI: 0.24-0.51, p < 0.001). Additionally, LA was associated with shorter hospital stays (SMD: -1.4 days, 95% CI: -2.3 to -0.5, p < 0.01) and reduced post-operative pain (OR = 0.57, 95% CI: 0.39-0.82, p = 0.02). There was no significant difference in the rates of bowel complications between LA and OA.

Conclusion: Our analysis suggests that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with lower wound infection rates compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. The findings support the use of minimally invasive surgery as a safe and effective alternative to traditional open appendectomy, reducing post-operative complications and improving patient outcomes.

Title: Comparative Efficacy of Laparoscopic vs Open Appendectomy on Wound Infection in Adults with Acute Appendicitis

Background:
Acute appendicitis is a common cause of acute abdomen, requiring surgical intervention. Open appendectomy (OA) has been the traditional treatment, but laparoscopic appendectomy (LA) offers advantages in terms of reduced morbidity and quicker recovery.

Methods:
We conducted a meta-analysis of 22 studies comparing LA to OA in adults with acute appendicitis. Eligible studies included patients aged ≥18 years with confirmed acute appendicitis undergoing elective or emergency surgery. We extracted data on wound infection rates, defined as clinical suspicion of surgical site infection (SSI) confirmed by laboratory tests and clinical examination.

Results:
Pooled analysis revealed a significant reduction in wound infection rate with LA compared to OA (RR: 0.43, 95% CI: 0.27-0.65). The standardized mean difference was -1.31 (95% CI: -2.13, -0.49), indicating a significant decrease in wound infection rates with LA. A subgroup analysis revealed that the benefit of LA on reducing wound infection was evident regardless of the study design (elective vs emergency).

Conclusion:
Our meta-analysis suggests that laparoscopic appendectomy significantly reduces wound infection compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis, with a relative risk of 0.43 (95% CI: 0.27-0.65) and standardized mean difference of -1.31 (95% CI: -2.13, -0.49). The findings support the use of laparoscopic approach in the treatment of acute appendicitis, particularly in reducing postoperative complications.

Title: Comparative Effectiveness of Laparoscopic versus Open Appendectomy on Wound Infection Rates in Adults with Acute Appendicitis

Background:
Acute appendicitis is a common gastrointestinal emergency that often requires surgical intervention. The choice between laparoscopic and open appendectomy has been debated due to concerns about wound infection rates.

Methods:
We conducted a randomized controlled trial (RCT) comparing the outcomes of 300 adults undergoing either laparoscopic or open appendectomy for acute appendicitis. Participants were recruited from two tertiary hospitals and were matched based on age, sex, and comorbidity status. The primary outcome was wound infection, defined as incisional or peritoneal infection occurring within 30 days post-surgery.

Results:
Laparoscopic appendectomy resulted in a significantly lower rate of wound infections compared to open appendectomy (3.4% vs. 12.1%, p < 0.001). The odds ratio for wound infection was 2.95 (95% CI: 1.83-4.86), indicating that laparoscopic appendectomy was associated with a 75% reduction in wound infection risk. Standardized mean difference of -3.53 (95% CI: -5.31 to -1.75) showed a significant difference between the two groups.

Conclusion:
This study provides evidence that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with significantly lower rates of wound infections compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. The findings suggest that laparoscopic surgery can reduce the risk of post-operative complications, supporting its use as the standard of care for acute appendicitis.

Title: Laparoscopic Appendectomy vs Open Appendectomy: A Systematic Review of Wound Infection Rates in Adults with Acute Appendicitis

Background: Acute appendicitis is a common cause of abdominal pain, and surgical treatment is often necessary. The choice between laparoscopic and open appendectomy has been debated, with concerns regarding wound infection rates.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review of 15 studies comparing laparoscopic appendectomy to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. Studies were included if they reported wound infection rates as primary or secondary outcomes.

Results: The pooled relative risk of wound infection from laparoscopic appendectomy compared to open appendectomy was 0.73 (95% CI, 0.55-0.93). The odds ratio for wound infection was also significantly lower in the laparoscopic group (OR 0.64, 95% CI, 0.48-0.86). Standardized mean difference comparing wound infection rates between groups was -0.24 (95% CI, -0.43 to -0.05). These findings suggest that laparoscopic appendectomy may be associated with a significantly lower risk of wound infection compared to open appendectomy.

Conclusion: Our systematic review suggests that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with a reduced risk of wound infection in adults with acute appendicitis, compared to open appendectomy. The evidence supports the use of laparoscopic appendectomy as a viable alternative for managing acute appendicitis, with potential benefits including decreased post-operative pain and faster recovery times. However, further studies are needed to confirm these findings and explore potential factors influencing wound infection rates in this population.

Title: Comparative Analysis of Laparoscopic Appendectomy vs Open Appendectomy in Adults with Acute Appendicitis

Background: Acute appendicitis is a common cause of abdominal pain and inflammation, requiring surgical intervention to prevent perforation and peritonitis. The choice between laparoscopic and open appendectomy has been debated due to concerns about wound infection.

Methods: A systematic review and meta-analysis were conducted to evaluate the effectiveness of laparoscopic appendectomy compared to open appendectomy in reducing wound infection among adults with acute appendicitis. Sixty-two studies (n=24,131) met the inclusion criteria. Pooled data from each study was analyzed using a random-effects model.

Results: The relative risk of wound infection after laparoscopic appendectomy was 0.53 (95% CI: 0.41-0.69), indicating a significant reduction in wound infection compared to open appendectomy. Specifically, the odds ratio for wound infection was 0.54 (95% CI: 0.37-0.78) and the standardized mean difference -0.43 (95% CI: -0.64 to -0.22). A subgroup analysis revealed that this benefit was consistent across different patient populations, including those with co-morbidities.

Conclusion: This meta-analysis provides strong evidence that laparoscopic appendectomy significantly reduces wound infection compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. The observed reduction in wound infection suggests that laparoscopic surgery may offer a safer alternative for patients undergoing surgical treatment of acute appendicitis.

Background: Acute appendicitis is a common cause of abdominal pain requiring surgical intervention. The choice between laparoscopic (mini-invasive) and open appendectomy has been debated due to concerns about wound complications, including infection.

Methods: This systematic review and meta-analysis included 15 studies comparing the incidence of wound infections in adults undergoing laparoscopic versus open appendectomy for acute appendicitis. We pooled data from 2,345 patients, with a total of 1,533 wound infections reported.

Results: Our analysis revealed a significant reduction in wound infection rates among patients undergoing laparoscopic appendectomy compared to open appendectomy (RR = 0.43, 95% CI: 0.24-0.74, p<0.001). The odds ratio was also significantly lower for laparoscopic appendectomy (OR = 0.42, 95% CI: 0.23-0.75, p=0.002), indicating a similar level of reduction in wound infections with either method.

Standardized mean difference (SMD) analysis revealed no significant differences between the two groups (SMD = -0.11, 95% CI: -0.35 to 0.14, p=0.79).

Conclusion: Our results suggest that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with a lower risk of wound infection compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. This finding supports the use of minimally invasive surgical techniques as a standard approach for treating acute appendicitis, potentially reducing postoperative complications and improving patient outcomes.

Conclusion: Our meta-analysis suggests that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with significantly lower wound infection rates compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. These findings support the use of minimally invasive surgical techniques as a first-line treatment option for acute appendicitis, potentially reducing the risk of post-operative complications and improving patient outcomes.

The pooled RR for wound infection in the laparoscopic group was 0.43 (95% CI: 0.29-0.62), indicating a significant reduction in wound infection compared to the open group (OR = 0.45, 95% CI: 0.31-0.64). The standardized mean difference (SMD) for wound infection rates was -0.51 (95% CI: -0.72 to -0.30), indicating a significant reduction in laparoscopic appendectomy.

Conclusion: This meta-analysis suggests that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with reduced wound infection compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis, supporting the use of minimally invasive surgical techniques as an effective strategy for reducing postoperative complications.

Title: Comparative Efficacy of Laparoscopic vs Open Appendectomy on Wound Infection Rates in Adults with Acute Appendicitis

Background: Surgical management of acute appendicitis is a common clinical scenario, with open appendectomy being the traditional approach. However, laparoscopic appendectomy has gained popularity due to its minimally invasive nature and potential benefits on postoperative recovery.

Methods: A systematic review and meta-analysis were conducted to compare wound infection rates between laparoscopic and open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. Databases including PubMed, Scopus, and Embase were searched for relevant studies published between 2000 and 2022. Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and cohort studies that reported wound infection rates were included.

Results: A total of 15 RCTs and 23 cohort studies met the inclusion criteria. The pooled results indicated a significant reduction in wound infection rates with laparoscopic appendectomy compared to open appendectomy (OR: 0.36, 95% CI: 0.18-0.73). Standardized mean difference (SMD) analysis also revealed a significant decrease in wound infection rates with laparoscopic appendectomy (-0.64, 95% CI: -1.11 to -0.17).

Conclusion: The results of this meta-analysis suggest that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with a reduced risk of wound infections compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. This finding has significant implications for the management of acute appendicitis, supporting the adoption of minimally invasive surgical techniques whenever feasible. However, further studies are needed to confirm these findings and explore potential biases or confounding variables that may have influenced the results.

We included studies that evaluated the incidence of wound infection as the primary outcome measure. A total of 12 RCTs were included in our analysis, involving 1,536 participants.

The reduced incidence of wound infections and complications may be attributed to the minimally invasive nature of laparoscopic surgery, which results in less tissue trauma and potentially fewer bacterial translocations. These findings support the use of laparoscopic appendectomy as a preferred treatment option for adult patients with acute appendicitis, provided that adequate postoperative care is ensured.

Title: Comparative Effectiveness of Laparoscopic vs Open Appendectomy on Wound Infection in Adults with Acute Appendicitis

Background: Acute appendicitis is a common cause of abdominal pain, and the standard treatment involves surgical appendectomy. The choice between laparoscopic and open appendectomy is often based on surgeon preference and institutional protocols. However, the risk of wound infection after surgery remains a significant concern.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing the outcomes of laparoscopic versus open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. We searched multiple databases, including PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane Library, for RCTs published between 1990 and 2022.

Results: A total of 12 RCTs involving 5,441 participants were included in the analysis. The pooled estimate of wound infection rates was significantly lower (RR = 0.43, 95% CI: 0.25-0.73) for laparoscopic appendectomy compared to open appendectomy (RR = 1.00). The odds ratio for wound infection was also reduced (OR = 0.48, 95% CI: 0.28-0.83). A standardized mean difference of -0.35 (95% CI: -0.62 to -0.08) was observed in favor of laparoscopic appendectomy.

Conclusion: Our analysis suggests that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with a significant reduction in wound infection rates compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. The findings support the use of minimally invasive surgery as a standard treatment option, potentially reducing the risk of postoperative complications and improving patient outcomes.

Title: Laparoscopic Appendectomy vs Open Appendectomy for Reducing Wound Infection in Adults with Acute Appendicitis

Background: Surgical appendectomy is a common treatment for acute appendicitis. The choice between laparoscopic and open surgery has been controversial, particularly regarding wound complications.

Methods: A systematic review and meta-analysis was conducted to compare the rates of wound infection after laparoscopic appendectomy (LA) versus open appendectomy (OA) in adults with acute appendicitis. Databases were searched for studies published from 1990 to 2022, using keywords such as "laparoscopic appendectomy," "open appendectomy," and "wound infection." The primary outcome was wound infection rate.

Results: A total of 24 studies were included, involving 10,313 patients. The pooled relative risk of wound infection after LA (RR=0.27, 95% CI: 0.14-0.52) and OA (RR=1.00 reference) indicated a significantly lower risk of wound infection with LA compared to OA.

Standardized mean difference (SMD) analysis revealed a significant reduction in wound infection rate with LA (-1.23 SD, 95% CI: -2.13 to -0.33). The odds ratio for wound infection after LA was also significantly lower than OA (OR=0.24, 95% CI: 0.11-0.53).

Conclusion: This meta-analysis suggests that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with a significant reduction in wound infection compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. The results support the use of minimally invasive surgical techniques for treating acute appendicitis, which may lead to improved patient outcomes and reduced healthcare costs.

Methods: A systematic review and meta-analysis were conducted on adult patients undergoing LA or OA for acute appendicitis. Databases including PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials were searched using keywords relevant to the study. Results were synthesized using random-effects models to calculate pooled relative risk (RR) of wound infection.

Results: A total of 13 studies with a combined sample size of 3,421 patients met the inclusion criteria. The overall RR for wound infection in LA compared to OA was 0.43 (95% CI: 0.24-0.74), indicating a significant reduction in wound infection risk with LA. Standardized mean difference (SMD) analysis also showed a significant SMD of -0.53 (95% CI: -0.76, -0.30).

Conclusion: The results of this systematic review and meta-analysis indicate that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with significantly lower rates of wound infection compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. This finding supports the use of LA as a viable alternative to OA for patients undergoing surgery for acute appendicitis, provided by an experienced surgeon.

Background: Surgical treatment for acute appendicitis is often performed using either laparoscopic or open techniques. The choice between these methods can significantly impact postoperative outcomes, including wound infection rates.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of 15 studies comparing laparoscopic appendectomy (LA) with open appendectomy (OA) in adults with acute appendicitis. Studies were identified through electronic databases and manual searches. Outcomes included wound infection rate, complication rates, and hospital stay duration.

Results: The overall pooled risk of wound infection was significantly lower for LA compared to OA (RR: 0.37, 95% CI: 0.23-0.58). The odds ratio for wound infection was also reduced in favor of LA (OR: 0.43, 95% CI: 0.26-0.71). Standardized mean difference (SMD) analyses revealed similar reductions in postoperative complications and hospital stay duration between the two groups.

Conclusion: Our systematic review and meta-analysis suggest that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with lower wound infection rates compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. These findings support the use of minimally invasive surgical techniques for the treatment of this condition, potentially leading to improved patient outcomes and reduced healthcare resource utilization. Further research is needed to confirm these results and explore potential explanations for the observed differences in postoperative outcomes between LA and OA.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of 15 randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing laparoscopic appendectomy to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. Outcomes included wound infection rate, defined as any postoperative wound complication requiring antibiotics or surgical intervention.

Results: Our analysis revealed that laparoscopic appendectomy was associated with a significantly lower risk of wound infection compared to open appendectomy (RR: 0.31, 95% CI: 0.23-0.43; OR: 0.35, 95% CI: 0.24-0.51). The standardized mean difference (SMD) for wound infection rate was -1.44 (95% CI: -2.25 to -0.63), indicating a significant reduction in wound infections with laparoscopic appendectomy. The majority of studies (12/15) demonstrated a reduction in wound complications, and the remaining three studies showed no significant difference.

Conclusion: Our findings suggest that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with a significantly lower risk of wound infection compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. This suggests that laparoscopic surgery may be an effective strategy for reducing postoperative wound complications, potentially improving patient outcomes and recovery rates. However, further studies are needed to confirm these findings and explore potential factors contributing to the reduced wound infection rate observed in laparoscopic appendectomies.

Background: Acute appendicitis is a common cause of abdominal pain requiring surgical intervention. The laparoscopic approach has gained popularity due to its minimally invasive nature, but evidence regarding its impact on wound infection rates compared to open appendectomy is limited.

Methods: A systematic review and meta-analysis were conducted in PubMed, Embase, and Scopus databases (January 2010 to December 2022). Studies comparing laparoscopic versus open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis were included. Outcomes of interest were wound infection rates, defined as surgical site infections (SSIs) or superficial incisional infections (SIIs).

Results: A total of 23 studies met the inclusion criteria, involving 3,432 patients (laparoscopic = 1,943; open = 1,489). Overall, the pooled odds ratio for laparoscopic appendectomy versus open appendectomy was 0.65 (95% CI: 0.49-0.87), indicating a reduced risk of wound infection with laparoscopic surgery. The standardized mean difference was -0.43 (95% CI: -0.63 to -0.23). Specifically, the rates of SSIs were significantly lower in the laparoscopic group (OR = 0.54, 95% CI: 0.38-0.78).

Conclusion: Laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with a reduced risk of wound infection compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. This finding supports the use of minimally invasive surgical techniques for managing this common condition, while minimizing postoperative complications.

Background: Acute appendicitis is a common gastrointestinal condition requiring surgical intervention. The choice between laparoscopic and open appendectomy has been debated, with concerns regarding wound infection rates. This study aimed to compare the wound infection rates between these two surgical approaches in adults with acute appendicitis.

Methods: A systematic review of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing laparoscopic and open appendectomy was conducted. Eligible studies included adult patients with confirmed acute appendicitis undergoing either laparoscopic or open appendectomy. Outcomes assessed were wound infection rates, defined as surgical site infections (SSIs) within 30 days post-surgery.

Results: A total of 17 RCTs involving 3,555 participants met the inclusion criteria. The pooled relative risk of wound infection in laparoscopic appendectomy was 0.59 (95% CI: 0.45-0.76), indicating a reduced risk of wound infections compared to open appendectomy.

Standardized mean difference (SMD) for wound infection rates between the two groups showed a significant reduction in favor of laparoscopic appendectomy, with an SMD of -0.53 (95% CI: -0.73, -0.33). The odds ratio for wound infections was 0.54 (95% CI: 0.38-0.76), also indicating a lower risk of wound infections in laparoscopic appendectomy.

Conclusion: This systematic review suggests that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with significantly reduced wound infection rates compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis, supporting its use as a preferred surgical option when feasible.

Title: Comparative Outcomes of Laparoscopic vs Open Appendectomy for Acute Appendicitis: A Systematic Review

Background: Acute appendicitis is a common gastrointestinal condition requiring prompt surgical intervention. The choice between laparoscopic (minimally invasive) and open appendectomy (traditional open surgery) has been a subject of debate regarding its impact on postoperative outcomes, including wound infection rates.

Methods: This systematic review and meta-analysis was conducted using multiple electronic databases to identify studies comparing laparoscopic and open appendectomy for acute appendicitis in adults. We included 22 studies that reported wound infection rates as outcome measures. The analysis was performed using a random-effects model to generate relative risk estimates.

Results: The pooled relative risk of wound infection after laparoscopic appendectomy compared to open appendectomy was 0.64 (95% CI, 0.49-0.85), indicating a reduced risk of wound infections in patients undergoing laparoscopic surgery.

Standardized mean difference analysis also revealed a significant reduction in wound infection rates with laparoscopic appendectomy (SMD = -0.26, 95% CI, -0.46 to -0.06).

Conclusion: Our findings suggest that laparoscopic appendectomy may reduce the risk of postoperative wound infections compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. The results support the growing evidence base for minimally invasive surgery, indicating a potential benefit for patients undergoing this procedure. However, further studies are needed to confirm these findings and explore other outcomes such as hospital stay length and complication rates.

Background: Acute appendicitis is a common surgical condition requiring prompt treatment. The choice between open and laparoscopic appendectomy has been debated due to concerns about wound infection rates.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing wound infection rates between laparoscopic and open appendectomies in adults with acute appendicitis. Eight RCTs were included, involving 1,434 participants.

Results: The pooled relative risk of wound infection after laparoscopic appendectomy was 0.63 (95% CI: 0.45-0.87), indicating a non-significant reduction compared to open appendectomy. However, the odds ratio for wound infection in laparoscopic appendectomy was 0.56 (95% CI: 0.38-0.80), suggesting a statistically significant reduction.

Standardized mean difference analysis revealed a significantly lower rate of wound infections in laparoscopic appendectomy (-0.23, 95% CI: -0.45 to -0.01). The pooled risk of wound infection was 7.4% (95% CI: 5.2-10.3%) after laparoscopic appendectomy and 15.6% (95% CI: 11.1-21.1%) after open appendectomy.

Conclusion: This meta-analysis suggests that laparoscopic appendectomy reduces wound infection rates compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis, with a non-significant relative risk reduction but a statistically significant odds ratio and standardized mean difference. The absolute differences were small, suggesting that the choice between laparoscopic and open appendectomy should be based on individual patient factors rather than surgical technique alone.

Title: Comparative Effectiveness of Laparoscopic vs Open Appendectomy on Wound Infection in Adults with Acute Appendicitis.

Background: Laparoscopic appendectomy has gained popularity for its minimally invasive approach, reducing postoperative pain and recovery time. However, the impact of laparoscopic surgery on wound infection rates compared to open appendectomy remains uncertain.

Methods: A prospective randomized controlled trial was conducted at a single center involving 200 adults with acute appendicitis. Participants were randomly assigned to either laparoscopic (n = 100) or open (n = 100) appendectomy groups. Wound infection rates were assessed using standardized wound assessment chart and surgical site infections were evaluated through wound swab culture.

Results: The primary outcome measure was wound infection rate, defined as a positive wound swab culture or clinical signs of infection within 30 days post-surgery. Overall, wound infection rates were lower in the laparoscopic group (6.5%) compared to the open group (15.1%, p < 0.01). The relative risk of wound infection was 0.43 (95% CI: 0.24-0.77), indicating a significant reduction in wound infection with laparoscopic appendectomy.

Conclusion: Our results demonstrate that laparoscopic appendectomy is associated with reduced wound infection rates compared to open appendectomy in adults with acute appendicitis. These findings support the use of minimally invasive surgical techniques as a preferred method for treating acute appendicitis, potentially reducing postoperative complications and improving patient outcomes.

Title: Effectiveness of Probiotic Co-Administration on Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common complication of antibiotic treatment, affecting up to 20% of patients. Probiotics have been proposed as a preventive measure for AAD due to their potential to restore the gut microbiota.

Methods: This randomized controlled trial included 200 adults undergoing systemic antibiotics for an infectious disease. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either a probiotic supplement (500 mg) or a placebo, alongside their antibiotic regimen. The primary outcome was AAD, defined as ≥3 episodes of loose stools per day during the treatment period.

Results: Probiotic co-administration significantly reduced the incidence of AAD compared to placebo (RR = 0.55, 95% CI: 0.37-0.80). Specifically, probiotics decreased the risk of AAD by 45% (OR = 1.27, 95% CI: 0.85-1.91) and reduced stool frequency by 30% (SMD = -0.25, 95% CI: -0.41 to -0.09). The probability of developing AAD was significantly lower in the probiotic group compared to the placebo group (p < 0.01).

Conclusion: Our findings suggest that co-administration of systemic antibiotics with a probiotic supplement can effectively prevent antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults. The observed reduction in AAD incidence and stool frequency supports the potential role of probiotics as a prophylactic measure for this complication. These results have implications for improving patient outcomes in infectious disease management, particularly for individuals at high risk of developing AAD.

Title: Probiotic Co-Administration Prevents Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common side effect of antibiotic therapy, affecting up to 50% of patients. Probiotics have been shown to prevent AAD in various studies, but the optimal regimen and population remain unclear.

Methods: We conducted a randomized controlled trial (RCT) involving 150 adults receiving systemic antibiotics for at least 7 days. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either probiotic co-treatment (Lactobacillus acidophilus and Bifidobacterium bifidum) or placebo, in addition to their standard antibiotic regimen.

Results: Probiotic co-administration significantly reduced the incidence of AAD compared to placebo (RR = 0.38, 95% CI: 0.24-0.60). The odds ratio for AAD was also lower with probiotic co-treatment (OR = 0.39, 95% CI: 0.25-0.62). Additionally, the standardized mean difference in stool frequency between groups was -2.5 (95% CI: -3.8 to -1.2).

Conclusion: Our findings suggest that co-administration of probiotics with systemic antibiotics can effectively prevent AAD in adults. The results support the use of probiotic supplements as a simple and effective strategy to reduce the burden of antibiotic-associated diarrhea, which is a significant concern for healthcare systems worldwide. Further studies are needed to establish optimal dosing regimens and long-term efficacy.

Title: Probiotic Co-Administration Reduces Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common adverse effect of systemic antibiotics, affecting up to 20% of patients. Probiotics have been investigated as a potential preventive measure against AAD.

Methods: We conducted a randomized controlled trial (RCT) involving 500 adults receiving systemic antibiotics for at least 7 days. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either probiotic supplements (100 billion CFU per day) or placebo for the duration of antibiotic therapy. Primary outcomes included incidence of diarrhea, defined as ≥3 loose stools in 24 hours.

Results: The probiotic group had a significantly lower incidence of diarrhea (14.5% vs. 32.1%, p<0.001) compared to the placebo group. The relative risk of developing diarrhea was 2.27 (95% CI: 1.63-3.87). Odds ratio for diarrhea was also reduced in the probiotic group (OR=0.44, 95% CI: 0.24-0.80).

Conclusion: Our results suggest that co-administration of probiotics with systemic antibiotics reduces the risk of antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults by approximately 57%. The exact mechanisms are unclear but may involve modulation of gut microbiota and immunomodulatory effects. These findings support the potential use of probiotics as a preventive strategy for AAD, particularly in high-risk populations such as older adults and those with compromised immune systems.

Conclusion: This RCT demonstrates that co-administration of probiotics with systemic antibiotics can effectively prevent antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults. Title: Probiotic Co-Administration Prevents Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common complication of systemic antibiotic therapy, affecting up to 20% of patients. The etiology of AAD is multifactorial and involves disruption of the gut microbiota.

Methods: This randomized controlled trial enrolled 500 adults receiving systemic antibiotics for various indications. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either probiotic co-administration (with Lactobacillus acidophilus, Bifidobacterium bifidum, or a combination) or placebo. Primary outcome was AAD defined as ≥3 loose stools within 48 hours post-antibiotic initiation.

Results: Probiotic co-administration significantly reduced the incidence of AAD (RR = 0.35, 95% CI: 0.20-0.60; p<0.01). Odds ratio for probiotic co-administration vs placebo was 0.36 (95% CI: 0.22-0.58; p=0.002). The risk difference for AAD between groups was 14.1% (95% CI: 8.4%, 20.3%; p<0.001).

Conclusion: Probiotic co-administration with systemic antibiotics significantly reduces the incidence of antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults, suggesting that this simple intervention may be a valuable adjunct to standard care for preventing this common complication. Our findings support the use of probiotics as an effective strategy for mitigating the adverse effects of antibiotics on gut health.

Background: Antibiotics can disrupt the gut microbiota, leading to antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD). Probiotics have been proposed as a potential adjunct therapy to prevent AAD.

Methods: We conducted a randomized controlled trial in adults receiving systemic antibiotics for at least 24 hours. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either probiotic supplementation or placebo. The primary outcome was the incidence of AAD, defined as loose or watery stools occurring during or within one week after antibiotic treatment.

Results: Of 240 participants (120 received probiotics and 120 received placebo), 62% developed AAD in the placebo group versus 26% in the probiotic group. The relative risk (RR) of AAD in the probiotic group was 0.42 (95% CI: 0.24, 0.73). Odds ratio (OR) for AAD was also significantly lower (OR = 0.38; 95% CI: 0.20, 0.72).

Standardized mean difference (SMD) for the incidence of AAD between groups was -0.62 (95% CI: -1.03, -0.21), indicating a significant reduction in AAD with probiotic supplementation. The majority of participants who developed AAD in both groups reported diarrhea occurring within 24 hours after antibiotic initiation.

Conclusion: Our findings suggest that co-administration of probiotics with systemic antibiotics may reduce the incidence of antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults, supporting its potential as an adjunct therapy to prevent this common adverse effect. Future studies should investigate the optimal timing and dosage of probiotic supplementation to further optimize its effectiveness.

Title: Probiotic Co-Administration Prevents Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common side effect of antibiotic treatment, affecting up to 30% of patients. The role of probiotics as adjunctive therapy remains unclear.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) examining the efficacy of probiotic co-administration on AAD in adults receiving systemic antibiotics. Eligible studies included participants with bacterial infections treated with broad-spectrum or narrow-spectrum antibiotics.

Results: Fifteen RCTs involving 1,543 participants were included in our analysis. Probiotic co-administration significantly reduced the incidence of AAD compared to antibiotic-only treatment (RR: 0.44, 95% CI: 0.24-0.83; p = 0.013). The odds ratio for probiotic co-administration was also significant (OR: 0.51, 95% CI: 0.31-0.84; p = 0.014).

Standardized mean difference of diarrhea episodes per participant-years between the probiotic and control groups was -0.53 (95% CI: -0.83 to -0.23; p < 0.001). The weighted average effect size suggested a moderate reduction in AAD incidence with probiotic co-administration.

Conclusion: Our meta-analysis suggests that probiotic co-administration significantly reduces the risk of antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults receiving systemic antibiotics. These findings support the use of probiotics as an adjunctive therapy to prevent AAD, potentially improving patient outcomes and reducing antibiotic resistance. Further studies are needed to investigate optimal probiotic strains and dosing regimens for this population.

Methods: This randomized controlled trial (RCT) evaluated the efficacy of probiotic co-administration in preventing AAD among adults receiving systemic antibiotics. The probability of AAD occurred in 12.1% of the placebo group versus 4.3% in the probiotic group (SMD=-7.5, 95% CI: -10.9 to -4.1).

Conclusion: Our results suggest that co-administration of systemic antibiotics with a specific probiotic supplement can effectively prevent antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults. The observed relative risk reduction and odds ratio indicate a significant association between probiotic supplementation and reduced incidence of AAD. These findings support the potential use of probiotics as an adjunct therapy to standard antibiotic regimens, particularly in high-risk populations such as elderly patients, immunocompromised individuals, or those with compromised gastrointestinal function.

Title: Probiotic Co-Administration Reduces Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis

Background: Antibiotics are widely used to treat bacterial infections, but they can disrupt the gut microbiota, leading to antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD). Probiotics have been proposed as a potential adjunct therapy to prevent AAD.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) examining the effect of probiotic co-administration with antibiotics on the incidence of AAD in adults. We included RCTs published between 2000 and 2022 and extracted data on outcomes, including the incidence of AAD, duration of treatment, and type of probiotics used.

Results: Our meta-analysis included 15 RCTs involving 1,433 participants. The results showed that probiotic co-administration significantly reduced the incidence of AAD (RR: 0.43, 95% CI: 0.26-0.72; OR: 0.46, 95% CI: 0.28-0.77) compared to antibiotic monotherapy alone. The pooled standardized mean difference in duration of treatment was also reduced by 2.3 days (95% CI: -3.1 to -1.5). We found no significant heterogeneity between studies.

Conclusion: Our systematic review and meta-analysis provide evidence that probiotic co-administration with antibiotics reduces the incidence of antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults receiving systemic antibiotics, suggesting a potential adjunctive therapy to prevent AAD. The results suggest that probiotics may help mitigate the adverse effects of antibiotics on the gut microbiota, but further research is needed to fully understand this association and to explore the optimal formulation and dosage of probiotics for use as an adjunct therapy.

Title: Efficacy of Probiotic Co-Administration in Preventing Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common complication of systemic antibiotic therapy, affecting up to 25% of patients. The introduction of probiotics as an adjunctive therapy has shown promise in reducing the incidence and severity of AAD.

Methods: We conducted a randomized controlled trial (RCT) involving adults receiving systemic antibiotics for at least 7 days. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either a probiotic supplement containing Lactobacillus acidophilus or Bifidobacterium bifidum, or a placebo. Primary outcomes included the incidence of AAD and clinical symptoms.

Results: Among 300 participants, 45 (15%) developed AAD in the control group versus 22 (7.3%) in the probiotic group (RR: 0.52, 95% CI: 0.34-0.79). The probiotic group showed a significant reduction in clinical symptoms of diarrhea (OR: 0.43, 95% CI: 0.25-0.75) and abdominal pain (aOR: 0.55, 95% CI: 0.35-0.86).

Conclusion: This RCT suggests that co-administration of probiotics with systemic antibiotics can prevent antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults. The observed reductions in AAD incidence and clinical symptoms support the potential benefits of probiotic therapy as an adjunctive measure to standard antibiotic treatment regimens.

Title: Effectiveness of Probiotic Co-Administration on Preventing Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common complication of systemic antibiotic therapy, affecting up to 20% of patients. Probiotics have been proposed as a potential adjunctive treatment to prevent AAD, but the evidence is limited and inconclusive.

Methods: We conducted a randomized controlled trial involving 300 adults receiving systemic antibiotics for an infectious disease or surgery. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either probiotics (containing Lactobacillus acidophilus and Bifidobacterium bifidum) or placebo, in addition to their standard antibiotic regimen. Primary outcome was AAD as defined by the presence of ≥3 loose stools or abdominal pain within 24-48 hours after the first dose of antibiotics.

Results: Probiotic co-administration significantly reduced the incidence of AAD compared to placebo (RR=0.45, 95% CI: 0.26-0.79; p=0.02). Odds ratio for probiotic co-administration was also lower among participants with antibiotic-resistant infections (OR=0.32, 95% CI: 0.14-0.74; p=0.01).

Conclusion: Our findings suggest that prophylactic co-administration of probiotics with systemic antibiotics in adults reduces the risk of AAD. The observed reduction was most pronounced among patients with antibiotic-resistant infections, implying potential benefits for high-risk populations. These results support the use of probiotics as an adjunctive treatment to prevent AAD and warrant further investigation into their role in preventing antibiotic-related complications.

Title: Probiotic Co-Administration Prevents Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common complication of systemic antibiotic therapy, affecting up to 20% of patients. The etiology of AAD is multifactorial and involves disruption of the gut microbiota.

Methods: We conducted a randomized controlled trial (RCT) involving 300 adults receiving systemic antibiotics for an infectious disease. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either probiotics (Lactobacillus acidophilus and Bifidobacterium bifidum) or placebo. The primary outcome was AAD, defined as diarrhea lasting ≥3 days post-antibiotic treatment.

Results: Probiotic co-administration significantly reduced the incidence of AAD compared to placebo (RR = 0.45, 95% CI: 0.24-0.82). Specifically, 15% of probiotic recipients developed AAD, while 40% of those receiving placebo experienced diarrhea. The odds ratio for developing AAD was 0.35 (95% CI: 0.16-0.78).

Standardized mean difference (SMD) analysis revealed a significant reduction in the duration and severity of diarrhea among probiotic recipients compared to placebo (SMD = -1.23, 95% CI: -1.85 to -0.61). Probiotic co-administration also reduced the need for antibiotic withdrawal due to gastrointestinal side effects.

Conclusion: Our findings suggest that prophylactic probiotic co-administration significantly reduces the incidence and severity of AAD in adults receiving systemic antibiotics. The observed benefits may be attributed to the restoration of the gut microbiota and improved barrier function against pathogenic bacteria. These results support the use of probiotics as an adjunctive therapy for preventing antibiotic-associated diarrhea.

abstract:
Title: Probiotic Co-Administration Prevents Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common complication of antibiotic therapy, affecting up to 30% of patients. The use of probiotics as an adjunct to antibiotics has been explored as a potential strategy for preventing AAD.

Methods: This systematic review and meta-analysis included 27 randomized controlled trials that investigated the effect of probiotic co-administration on the incidence of AAD in adults receiving systemic antibiotics. Outcomes were measured using the frequency and duration of diarrhea episodes, stool quality, and clinical symptoms.

Results: The pooled relative risk of developing AAD was significantly reduced (RR=0.65, 95% CI: 0.51-0.82) when probiotics were co-administered with antibiotics. Specifically, Lactobacillus acidophilus GG and Bifidobacterium bifidum subsp. lactis BB-12 demonstrated the most significant protective effects (OR=0.44, 95% CI: 0.33-0.57). The standardized mean difference in diarrhea frequency was -0.35 (95% CI: -0.54 to -0.16), indicating a significant reduction in AAD episodes with probiotic co-administration.

Conclusion: This systematic review suggests that the co-administration of probiotics with systemic antibiotics can significantly reduce the incidence and duration of antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults. The most effective probiotic strains appear to be Lactobacillus acidophilus GG and Bifidobacterium bifidum subsp. lactis BB-12. These findings support the use of probiotics as an adjunct therapy for preventing AAD, particularly in high-risk populations such as hospital-acquired infection patients and immunocompromised individuals.

Title: Probiotic Co-Administration Reduces Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common complication of systemic antibiotic therapy, causing significant morbidity and mortality. Probiotics have been proposed as a potential adjunctive therapy to prevent AAD; however, evidence is limited.

Methods: We conducted a randomized controlled trial (RCT) involving 240 adults receiving systemic antibiotics for ≥5 days. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either probiotic supplementation (Lactobacillus acidophilus and Bifidobacterium bifidum) or placebo for the duration of antibiotic therapy. Diarrhea incidence was assessed using standardized criteria.

Results: Probiotic co-administration resulted in a significant reduction in AAD incidence (RR = 0.43, 95% CI: 0.23-0.79; p = 0.05). Odds ratio for probiotic co-administration versus placebo was 0.38 (95% CI: 0.18-0.80; p = 0.02). Standardized mean difference in diarrhea incidence between the intervention and control groups was -0.46 (95% CI: -0.73 to -0.19; p = 0.001).

Conclusion: Our findings suggest that probiotic co-administration may prevent AAD in adults receiving systemic antibiotics. The results of this RCT support the use of probiotics as an adjunctive therapy for reducing antibiotic-associated diarrhea, and further research is warranted to explore its clinical utility in various patient populations.

Title: Probiotic Co-Administration Prevents Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common adverse effect of systemic antibiotic therapy, affecting up to 50% of patients. Probiotics have been proposed as a potential adjunctive treatment to prevent AAD; however, evidence remains limited and inconsistent.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) evaluating the effectiveness of probiotic co-administration with systemic antibiotics in preventing AAD. Eight RCTs involving 1,434 participants were included.

Results: Our analysis found that probiotic co-administration significantly reduced the risk of AAD (RR: 0.33, 95% CI: 0.19-0.57), with a moderate heterogeneity between studies (I^2 = 44.3%). The odds ratio for AAD prevention was also significant (OR: 0.37, 95% CI: 0.23-0.59). When analyzing the standardized mean difference (SMD) of probiotic co-administration on the risk of AAD in comparison to placebo, we found a moderate effect size (SMD: -0.43, 95% CI: -0.69 to -0.17).

Conclusion: Our meta-analysis suggests that probiotic co-administration with systemic antibiotics can effectively prevent antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults, with a significant reduction in risk and moderate heterogeneity between studies. These findings support the use of probiotics as an adjunctive treatment strategy for AAD prevention, warranting further investigation into the optimal probiotic strains and dosages for this purpose.

Title: Probiotic Co-Administration Prevents Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common complication of antibiotic therapy, affecting up to 30% of patients. The etiology of AAD is multifactorial and involves disruption of the gut microbiota.

Methods: We conducted a randomized controlled trial (RCT) involving 500 adults receiving systemic antibiotics for at least 5 days. Participants were randomly allocated to receive either probiotic co-administration (10^6 CFU of Lactobacillus acidophilus and Bifidobacterium bifidum per day) or placebo for the duration of antibiotic therapy.

Results: The primary outcome was the development of AAD, defined as ≥ 2 loose stools per day. Probiotic co-administration significantly reduced the risk of developing AAD compared to placebo (RR: 0.44, 95% CI: 0.27-0.75). Additionally, probiotic co-administration reduced the incidence of Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI), a common cause of AAD (OR: 0.42, 95% CI: 0.24-0.73).

Conclusion: Our findings suggest that co-administering probiotics with systemic antibiotics can prevent antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults. The reduced risk of AAD and CDI suggests that probiotic co-administration may help mitigate the disruption of the gut microbiota caused by antibiotics, thereby reducing the risk of infection. These results have significant implications for clinical practice, suggesting that prophylactic use of probiotics may be a useful adjunct to antibiotic therapy in high-risk patients.

Title: Efficacy of Probiotic Co-Administration in Preventing Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.

Background: Antibiotics are a common cause of diarrhea, particularly Clostridioides difficile (C. diff) infection. The use of probiotics as an adjunctive therapy to prevent antibiotic-associated diarrhea has been proposed, but evidence is limited and inconsistent.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that evaluated the efficacy of probiotic co-administration in preventing antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults receiving systemic antibiotics. We searched multiple databases, including PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science, using relevant keywords and inclusion criteria.

Results: Eight RCTs were included in our analysis, involving a total of 2,115 participants. The pooled relative risk (RR) of developing antibiotic-associated diarrhea was significantly reduced by 44% (95% CI: 0.56-0.65) with probiotic co-administration. The odds ratio (OR) for C. diff infection was also reduced by 55% (95% CI: 0.21-0.43). However, the standardized mean difference (SMD) between the probiotic group and control group for diarrhea incidence was not statistically significant.

Conclusion: Our meta-analysis suggests that co-administration of probiotics with antibiotics in adults may prevent antibiotic-associated diarrhea, with a moderate reduction in risk. The findings support the potential use of probiotics as an adjunctive therapy to reduce the incidence of diarrhea and C. diff infection associated with antibiotic treatment.

Title: Efficacy of Probiotic Co-Administration in Preventing Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults

Background: Antibiotics are commonly prescribed to treat various bacterial infections, but they can also disrupt the gut microbiota, leading to antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD). Probiotics have been proposed as a potential adjunctive therapy to prevent AAD.

Methods: This randomized controlled trial enrolled 240 adults receiving systemic antibiotics for at least 24 hours. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either probiotics (5 x 10^8 CFU of Lactobacillus acidophilus and Bifidobacterium bifidum) or a placebo, in addition to their standard antibiotic regimen.

Results: The primary outcome was the incidence of AAD, defined as diarrhea lasting more than 3 days. Secondary outcomes included stool frequency, stool consistency, and presence of blood in stool. The results showed that probiotic co-administration significantly reduced the risk of developing AAD (RR = 0.45, 95% CI: 0.24-0.83).

Standardized mean difference (SMD) with 95% confidence interval was not applicable due to zero events. Odds ratio for stool frequency and presence of blood in stool were 0.68 (95% CI: 0.45-0.98) and 0.58 (95% CI: 0.35-0.97), respectively.

Conclusion: This study provides evidence that co-administration of probiotics with systemic antibiotics can prevent antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults. The results suggest a potential benefit of using probiotics as an adjunctive therapy to reduce the risk of AAD, which is associated with significant morbidity and mortality. Further studies are needed to confirm these findings and explore the optimal dosing and strain selection for prophylactic use.

Title: Probiotic Co-Administration Prevents Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea is a common complication of systemic antibiotic therapy. While antibiotics can effectively treat bacterial infections, they disrupt the gut microbiome, leading to diarrhea and other gastrointestinal symptoms.

Methods: This randomized controlled trial (RCT) recruited 300 adults receiving systemically administered antibiotics for at least 5 days. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either probiotic supplementation (10 billion CFU/day of Lactobacillus acidophilus and Bifidobacterium bifidum) or a placebo, alongside their antibiotic therapy.

Results: Probiotic co-administration was found to significantly reduce the incidence of antibiotic-associated diarrhea compared to placebo. The pooled relative risk (RR) was 0.31 (95% CI: 0.15-0.81), indicating a significant reduction in diarrhea risk. Additionally, the odds ratio (OR) for probiotic use and reduced diarrhea risk was 0.38 (95% CI: 0.18-0.84).

Standardized mean difference (SMD) analyses revealed that probiotic supplementation resulted in a significant decrease in stool frequency (-2.3 ± 1.2 events/week, 95% CI: -4.6 to -0.01), as well as a reduction in symptom severity (OR for reduced symptoms: 0.55, 95% CI: 0.34-0.89).

Conclusion: This study demonstrates that co-administering probiotics with systemically administered antibiotics effectively reduces the incidence and severity of antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults. The findings support the use of probiotic supplementation as a simple and non-invasive adjunctive strategy to mitigate this common adverse effect of antibiotic therapy.

abstract:
Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common side effect of systemic antibiotics, affecting up to 20% of patients. Probiotics have been shown to prevent AAD in various studies, but the evidence is inconsistent. This meta-analysis aimed to investigate whether co-administration of probiotics with systemic antibiotics prevents AAD in adults.

Methods: We searched multiple databases for randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that compared antibiotic treatment with or without probiotic supplementation. Inclusion criteria were adult patients receiving systemic antibiotics and experiencing AAD. Outcomes included incidence of AAD, duration of diarrhea, and clinical symptoms. We extracted effect estimates using relative risk (RR), odds ratio (OR), and standardized mean difference (SMD).

Results: 15 RCTs involving 2,345 participants met our criteria. Probiotic co-administration significantly reduced the incidence of AAD by 45% (RR = 0.55, 95% CI: 0.41-0.72). The pooled SMD for duration of diarrhea was -1.23 days (95% CI: -2.03 to -0.43), indicating a significant reduction in duration. Clinical symptoms such as abdominal pain and nausea were also reduced.

Conclusion: Our meta-analysis suggests that co-administration of probiotics with systemic antibiotics significantly reduces the incidence of antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults, with moderate reductions in duration and clinical symptoms. The results support the use of probiotics as an adjunctive treatment to prevent AAD, particularly in high-risk populations such as hospitalized patients or those undergoing chemotherapy.

abstract:
Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common adverse effect of systemic antibiotics, affecting up to 50% of patients. Probiotics have been investigated as a potential preventive measure, but evidence remains limited.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) examining the effect of probiotic co-administration on AAD in adults receiving systemic antibiotics. Eligible studies included RCTs with a control group, where probiotics were either administered alone or in combination with antibiotics.

Results: Fourteen RCTs involving 1,236 participants met our inclusion criteria. Probiotic supplementation was associated with a significant reduction in the incidence of AAD (RR: 0.34, 95% CI: 0.21-0.53; OR: 0.42, 95% CI: 0.24-0.73). The pooled odds ratio for Lactobacillus acidophilus (L. acidophilus) and Bifidobacterium bifidum (B. bifidum) supplementation was 0.44 (95% CI: 0.25-0.79), with a standardized mean difference of -0.63 (95% CI: -1.04 to -0.22).

Conclusion: Our meta-analysis suggests that probiotic co-administration with systemic antibiotics can prevent AAD in adults. The results were consistent across multiple probiotic strains, particularly L. acidophilus and B. bifidum. These findings support the use of probiotics as a preventive measure for AAD in patients receiving antibiotics, but further research is needed to establish optimal dosing regimens and long-term efficacy.

Title: Efficacy of Probiotic Co-Administration in Preventing Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics

Background:
Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common complication of antibiotic therapy, affecting up to 20% of patients. Probiotics have been suggested as a potential adjunctive treatment to prevent AAD, but evidence is limited.

Methods:
This systematic review and meta-analysis included randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that investigated the efficacy of probiotic co-administration with systemic antibiotics in preventing AAD. We searched multiple electronic databases up to 2022 and included studies published in English.

Results:
Eleven RCTs were included, involving a total of 1,434 participants. Probiotic co-administration was found to significantly reduce the risk of AAD (RR: 0.34, 95% CI: 0.16-0.71; p=0.001), with an odds ratio of 0.43 (95% CI: 0.23-0.93; p=0.023) and a standardized mean difference of -0.57 (95% CI: -0.83 to -0.31; p<0.001). The most commonly used probiotic strains were Lactobacillus acidophilus and Bifidobacterium bifidum.

Conclusion:
Probiotic co-administration with systemic antibiotics is effective in preventing antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults. The results suggest that probiotics may be a useful adjunctive treatment to reduce the incidence of AAD, particularly in high-risk populations such as older adults or those with compromised immune systems. Further studies are needed to confirm these findings and explore optimal probiotic strain selection and dosing regimens for this indication.

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common complication of systemic antibiotic therapy, affecting up to 20% of patients. Probiotics have been proposed as a preventive measure, but evidence is limited.

Methods: We conducted a randomized controlled trial (RCT) in adults receiving systemic antibiotics for at least 3 days. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either a probiotic supplement (500 million CFU per day) or a placebo, alongside their antibiotic therapy. The primary outcome was the development of AAD, defined as loose stools or diarrhea lasting ≥2 days during or within 30 days of antibiotic treatment.

Results: Of 300 participants enrolled, 146 (48.7%) developed AAD. Probiotic co-administration significantly reduced the risk of developing AAD (RR=0.45; 95% CI: 0.22-0.94), with a relative reduction of 52%. The odds ratio for AAD was also lower in the probiotic group (OR=0.41; 95% CI: 0.18-0.92). Standardized mean difference (SMD) analysis revealed a significant SMD (-1.14; 95% CI: -2.00 to -0.28) for the primary outcome, indicating a greater protective effect of probiotics.

Conclusion: Our RCT demonstrates that co-administration of probiotic supplements with systemic antibiotics significantly reduces the risk of antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults. The observed relative reduction and odds ratio suggest that probiotics may be an effective adjunctive measure to prevent AAD, potentially improving patient outcomes and reducing antibiotic use.

Title: Probiotic Co-Administration Prevents Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea is a common complication of antibiotic therapy, affecting up to 20% of patients. The role of probiotics as an adjunctive treatment for preventing this condition has been explored.

Methods: A randomized controlled trial was conducted involving 500 adults receiving systemic antibiotics for at least 7 days. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either a standard antibiotic regimen alone or in combination with a probiotic supplement containing Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG and Bifidobacterium bifidum. The primary outcome measure was the incidence of antibiotic-associated diarrhea, defined as loose stools occurring within 24 hours after the start of antibiotic therapy.

Results: The incidence of antibiotic-associated diarrhea in the standard antibiotic group (42%) was significantly lower than in the probiotic co-administration group (62%). Relative risk (RR) was 0.65 (95% CI: 0.46-0.92, p=0.012). Odds ratio (OR) was also reduced to 0.59 (95% CI: 0.39-0.87, p=0.010).

Conclusion: Our results suggest that co-administering probiotics with systemic antibiotics in adults reduces the risk of antibiotic-associated diarrhea by approximately 35%. This finding supports the potential benefits of probiotic supplementation as an adjunctive treatment to mitigate the adverse effects of antibiotic therapy. Future studies should investigate the long-term efficacy and safety of probiotic co-administration in different populations and disease settings.

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common complication of systemic antibiotic therapy, affecting up to 20% of patients. Probiotics have been explored as a potential preventive measure for AAD.

Methods: We conducted a randomized controlled trial in adults receiving systemic antibiotics. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either probiotics or no probiotics. The primary outcome was the incidence of AAD, defined as diarrhea lasting ≥3 days. Secondary outcomes included symptom severity and duration.

Results: 300 participants were enrolled (150 received probiotics, 150 did not). The primary outcome analysis revealed a significant reduction in AAD incidence with probiotic co-administration (RR 0.53, 95% CI 0.34-0.82), with an odds ratio of 0.55 (95% CI 0.35-0.86) and standardized mean difference -0.41 (95% CI -0.64 to -0.18). Probiotic co-administration also significantly reduced symptom severity (-1.2 points on a 10-point scale, 95% CI -2.3 to -0.1) and duration.

Conclusion: Our findings suggest that probiotic co-administration with systemic antibiotics may effectively prevent AAD in adults, reducing incidence by approximately 47%. These results have important implications for the prevention of antibiotic-related complications in clinical practice. Further research is needed to confirm these findings and explore potential mechanisms underlying the prophylactic effects of probiotics.

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common adverse effect of systemic antibiotics, affecting up to 20% of patients. Probiotics have been proposed as an adjunct therapy to prevent AAD.

Methods: We conducted a randomized controlled trial in adults receiving systemic antibiotics for at least 3 days. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either a probiotic supplement (containing Lactobacillus acidophilus and Bifidobacterium bifidum) or placebo, both starting on day 1 of antibiotic therapy.

Results: There was no significant difference in the incidence of AAD between groups (RR = 1.01, 95% CI: 0.88-1.15). Odds ratio for AAD was also not significantly different between groups (OR = 1.04, 95% CI: 0.79-1.36). However, we observed a significant reduction in Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI) incidence in the probiotic group compared to placebo (RR = 0.46, 95% CI: 0.23-0.93).

Conclusion: Our findings suggest that co-administration of probiotics with systemic antibiotics does not significantly prevent antibiotic-associated diarrhea. However, we observed a notable reduction in CDI incidence, which may be attributed to the beneficial effects of probiotics on gut microbiota and immune system modulation. Future studies should investigate the mechanisms underlying this protective effect and explore potential benefits of probiotic co-therapy for preventing CDI in high-risk populations.

Title: Efficacy of Probiotic Co-Administration in Preventing Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common complication of antibiotic therapy, affecting up to 20% of patients. Probiotics have been proposed as an adjunctive treatment to prevent AAD; however, the evidence is limited.

Methods: We conducted a randomized controlled trial (RCT) involving 1,200 adults receiving systemic antibiotics for at least 7 days. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either probiotics (Lactobacillus acidophilus and Bifidobacterium bifidum) or placebo. The primary outcome was AAD defined as ≥3 episodes of diarrhea per day. We analyzed the data using intention-to-treat analysis.

Results: Probiotic co-administration significantly reduced the incidence of AAD (RR=0.33, 95% CI: 0.24-0.45), with a relative risk reduction of 65%. Odds ratio for probiotic co-administration vs. placebo was also significant (OR=0.27, 95% CI: 0.16-0.46). The standardized mean difference in diarrhea episodes per day between the probiotic and placebo groups was -1.43 (95% CI: -2.15 to -0.71).

Conclusion: This RCT provides strong evidence that probiotic co-administration significantly reduces the incidence of antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults receiving systemic antibiotics, with a relative risk reduction of 65%. The findings support the use of probiotics as an adjunctive treatment for preventing AAD and suggest potential benefits for patients undergoing antibiotic therapy.

Title: Probiotic Co-Administration Prevents Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Adults Receiving Systemic Antibiotics: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis

Background: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) is a common side effect of systemic antibiotic therapy, affecting up to 20% of patients. The use of probiotics as an adjunct therapy has been proposed to prevent AAD, but the evidence is inconsistent.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that investigated the efficacy of probiotic co-administration with systemic antibiotics in preventing AAD in adults. We searched multiple databases and included 12 RCTs with a total of 1,444 participants.

Results: The pooled relative risk of developing AAD in the probiotic group compared to the control group was 0.44 (95% CI: 0.31-0.62). The odds ratio was 0.43 (95% CI: 0.29-0.61), and the standardized mean difference was -0.56 (95% CI: -0.81, -0.31) in favor of probiotic co-administration. The risk reduction for AAD was estimated to be 55% (95% CI: 44%-65%).

Conclusion: Our meta-analysis suggests that probiotic co-administration with systemic antibiotics significantly reduces the incidence of antibiotic-associated diarrhea in adults. The results support the use of probiotics as an adjunct therapy to prevent AAD, and further studies are needed to confirm these findings and explore potential mechanisms of action.
