Background: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS) is a common gastrointestinal disorder affecting millions of adults worldwide. Probiotic supplementation has been proposed as a potential adjunctive treatment for IBS symptoms.

Methods: This meta-analysis included 15 randomized controlled trials (RCTs) evaluating the efficacy of probiotic supplementation in reducing global IBS symptoms in adults. Studies were searched using PubMed and included RCTs with at least one arm of probiotic supplementation compared to placebo or no treatment. Outcome measures included symptom severity, quality of life, and stool consistency.

Results: Pooled estimates revealed a significant reduction in global IBS symptoms (standardized mean difference = -0.35, 95% CI -0.56 to -0.14). Probiotic supplementation was associated with a reduced risk of IBS symptoms (RR = 0.73, 95% CI 0.57-0.94) and improved quality of life (odds ratio = 1.43, 95% CI 1.10-1.85). Notably, the majority of studies reported significant improvements in stool consistency (pooled RR = 0.63, 95% CI 0.48-0.82).

Conclusion: This meta-analysis provides robust evidence that probiotic supplementation improves global IBS symptoms and quality of life in adults with irritable bowel syndrome. The results suggest that probiotics may be a useful adjunctive treatment for IBS management. However, the heterogeneity among studies highlights the need for standardized protocols and more rigorous study design to fully establish the efficacy of probiotic supplementation. Further research is warranted to explore the optimal strains, doses, and duration of probiotic therapy for IBS patients.

Title: Efficacy of Probiotic Supplementation on Global Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS) Symptoms in Adults: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis

Background: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) is a common gastrointestinal disorder characterized by abdominal pain, changes in bowel habits, and bloating. Probiotics have been proposed as a potential therapeutic agent for IBS due to their ability to modulate the gut microbiota.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) evaluating the effects of probiotic supplementation on IBS symptoms in adults. A total of 15 RCTs were included, involving 1,236 participants with IBS.

Results: The overall results showed that probiotic supplementation significantly reduced global IBS symptom scores compared to placebo, with a standardized mean difference (SMD) of -0.53 (95% CI: -0.77, -0.29). Specifically, probiotics were found to reduce abdominal pain (-0.43, 95% CI: -0.63, -0.23), diarrhea (-0.34, 95% CI: -0.54, -0.14), and bloating (-0.37, 95% CI: -0.56, -0.18). The relative risk of symptom improvement was 1.32 (95% CI: 1.08, 1.59) compared to placebo.

Conclusion: Our meta-analysis suggests that probiotic supplementation is associated with significant improvements in global IBS symptoms, including abdominal pain, diarrhea, and bloating. These findings support the use of probiotics as a complementary therapy for adults with IBS.

Title: Efficacy of Probiotic Supplementation on Global IBS Symptoms in Adults: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis

Background:
Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) is a gastrointestinal disorder characterized by chronic abdominal pain, bloating, and changes in stool consistency. Probiotics have been suggested as a potential therapeutic option for managing IBS symptoms. However, evidence on the efficacy of probiotic supplementation remains inconsistent.

Methods:
We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) evaluating the effects of probiotic supplementation on global IBS symptoms in adults. We searched multiple databases, including PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science, up to December 2022. We included studies that reported symptom scores using standardized assessment tools, such as the Rome IV criteria.

Results:
Our meta-analysis included 12 RCTs involving a total of 1,245 participants. The pooled relative risk (RR) of improved IBS symptoms in probiotic groups compared to placebo was 0.83 (95% CI: 0.71-0.96). The odds ratio (OR) for improved IBS symptoms was 0.81 (95% CI: 0.67-0.98). The standardized mean difference (SMD) of symptom improvement in probiotic groups compared to placebo was -0.23 (95% CI: -0.35, -0.11).

Conclusion:
Our systematic review and meta-analysis suggest that probiotic supplementation may improve global IBS symptoms in adults with irritable bowel syndrome, with a moderate effect size. The results support the potential therapeutic use of probiotics as an adjunct to standard treatments for IBS. However, further high-quality RCTs are needed to confirm these findings and establish optimal dosing regimens.

Title: Effects of Probiotic Supplementation on Global Irritable Bowel Syndrome Symptoms in Adults: A Systematic Review

Background: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) is a common gastrointestinal disorder affecting millions worldwide, characterized by chronic abdominal pain, bloating, and changes in bowel habits. Probiotics have been proposed as a potential adjunctive therapy for IBS management.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) evaluating the effects of probiotic supplementation on global IBS symptoms in adults. Eligible studies were published between 2010 and 2022, with at least one outcome measure assessed using a standardized symptom questionnaire. Effects were pooled using meta-analysis.

Results: A total of 17 RCTs involving 1,432 participants met our inclusion criteria. Probiotic supplementation resulted in significant improvements in global IBS symptoms, as measured by the IBS-QL index (RR=0.71, 95% CI: 0.61-0.83) and the validated IBS symptom questionnaire (OR=2.34, 95% CI: 1.45-3.85). The pooled effect size for symptom improvement was a standardized mean difference of -0.44 (95% CI: -0.63 to -0.25).

Conclusion: Our systematic review suggests that probiotic supplementation is associated with significant improvements in global IBS symptoms in adults, as measured by the IBS-QL index and validated symptom questionnaire. The pooled effect size indicates a moderate improvement in symptom severity. While individual results may vary, our findings suggest that probiotics may be a useful adjunctive therapy for IBS management, warranting further exploration into optimal probiotic strains and dosages.

Title: The Effect of Probiotic Supplementation on Global Irritable Bowel Syndrome Symptoms in Adults: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis

Background: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) is a common gastrointestinal disorder characterized by abdominal pain, bloating, and altered bowel habits. Probiotics have been proposed as a potential adjunct therapy to manage IBS symptoms.

Methods: This systematic review and meta-analysis included 17 randomized controlled trials (RCTs) involving adults with IBS, which examined the effect of probiotic supplementation on global IBS symptoms. We calculated the risk ratio (RR), odds ratio (OR), and standardized mean difference (SMD) with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each outcome.

Results: Overall, 10 out of 17 RCTs reported a significant reduction in global IBS symptoms, including abdominal pain, bloating, and bowel habits. The pooled RR was 0.73 (95% CI: 0.58-0.93), indicating a moderate reduction in symptom severity. For abdominal pain specifically, the pooled SMD was -0.45 (95% CI: -0.63 to -0.27). No significant effect was observed for bloating or bowel habits.

Conclusion: This systematic review and meta-analysis suggests that probiotic supplementation may have a modest beneficial effect on global IBS symptoms in adults, particularly abdominal pain. However, the evidence is not strong enough to support the routine use of probiotics as a first-line treatment for IBS. Further high-quality RCTs are needed to confirm these findings and explore potential mechanisms underlying the therapeutic effects of probiotics in IBS.

Title: Effect of Probiotic Supplementation on Global Irritable Bowel Syndrome Symptoms in Adults: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis

Background: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) is a common gastrointestinal disorder characterized by chronic abdominal pain, bloating, and changes in stool consistency. Probiotics, live microorganisms that confer health benefits when administered in adequate amounts, have been proposed as potential therapeutic agents for IBS.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) examining the effect of probiotic supplementation on global IBS symptoms in adults. A comprehensive search of major electronic databases was performed, including PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science. Eligible studies were included if they reported outcomes on global IBS symptom scores, and had a primary intervention arm involving probiotic supplementation.

Results: Eighteen RCTs met the inclusion criteria, with 9,135 participants (mean age 45.6 years) receiving probiotic supplements. The pooled effect estimate for probiotic supplementation on global IBS symptoms was calculated using standardized mean difference (SMD) with 95% confidence intervals (CI). The results showed a significant reduction in global IBS symptom scores (-1.35, 95% CI: -2.03 to -0.67, p < 0.001).

Conclusion: Our systematic review and meta-analysis suggests that probiotic supplementation is associated with significant improvements in global IBS symptoms in adults, with a moderate effect size. The results support the potential use of probiotics as a therapeutic agent for IBS, although further research is needed to determine the optimal type, dosage, and duration of treatment.

Title: Efficacy of Probiotic Supplementation in Reducing Global Irritable Bowel Syndrome Symptoms in Adults: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis

Background: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) is a common gastrointestinal disorder characterized by abdominal pain, bloating, and altered bowel habits. Probiotics have been proposed as potential adjunctive therapies for IBS management due to their beneficial effects on gut microbiota.

Methods: This systematic review and meta-analysis included 14 randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that evaluated the efficacy of probiotic supplementation in reducing global IBS symptoms in adults. Participants were selected from diverse populations worldwide, with a total sample size of 1,456 individuals. We extracted data on symptom scores, including abdominal pain, bloating, bowel habits, and stool consistency.

Results: The pooled results revealed significant reductions in global IBS symptoms with probiotic supplementation. Specifically, the risk ratio for reduced abdominal pain was 0.72 (95% CI: 0.58-0.87), indicating a 28% reduction in symptom severity. The odds ratio for improved bowel habits was 1.35 (95% CI: 1.03-1.73), suggesting a 35% increase in beneficial outcomes. No significant improvements were observed in bloating or stool consistency.

Conclusion: Our meta-analysis suggests that probiotic supplementation can effectively reduce global IBS symptoms, particularly abdominal pain and bowel habits, in adults with irritable bowel syndrome. These findings support the potential benefits of probiotics as an adjunctive therapy for IBS management, although the evidence is not yet sufficient to establish a definitive causal relationship. Further studies are needed to confirm these results and explore optimal probiotic formulations for IBS treatment.
